_note: Real ChatGPT re-audit on v3. Contradicts Gemini v3 (which gave bias_score 0 with all-empty flag arrays) — ChatGPT found 7 cardinal-sin candidates in v3, several of which v4 must address before publication.

claims.claim: Singapore charged three men in Feb 2025 over servers.
claims.correction: Accurate, but charge was fraud by false representation against suppliers of servers; SPF release does not itself confirm Nvidia chips or Malaysian end-use.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue as of 18 May 2026.

claims.claim: The three charged on 27 Feb 2025 were a Singaporean, a permanent resident, and a Chinese national.
claims.correction: Authoritative reporting identifies two Singaporeans, Aaron Woon Guo Jie and Alan Wei Zhaolun, and one Chinese national, Li Ming. No support for describing one as a permanent resident.

claims.claim: A fourth individual was charged on 2 April 2026.
claims.correction: Accurate. Fourth: Lim Jenny, CFO of Aperia International Pte Ltd; alleged conspiracy with Alan Wei and Aaron Woon re Dell.

claims.claim: Singapore's Minister Shanmugam said the servers possibly carried Nvidia chips and moved to Malaysia; the final destination was unknown.
claims.correction: Safest wording: Singapore assessed the servers may contain Nvidia chips, knew the servers went to Malaysia, did not know the final destination.

claims.claim: No Malaysian operator faces charges yet.
claims.source: SPF releases name Singapore-linked accused only; MITI/MY enforcement searches as of 18 May 2026 reveal no public Malaysian operator charge.
claims.correction: Phrase as: 'I found no public enforcement release naming a Malaysian operator as of 18 May 2026; non-public investigations cannot be ruled out.'

claims.claim: The US 'Tier 2' label came from a rule that never officially named Malaysia.
claims.source: Federal Register 90 Fed. Reg. 4544; BIS press release 13 May 2025
claims.correction: Rule text didn't name Malaysia. However, 'second-tier status' appears in BIS's 13 May 2025 rescission announcement, not as a literal Malaysia-specific label.

claims.claim: The AI Diffusion Rule was paused before it took effect.
claims.source: GAO B-337935, 12 May 2026; BIS press release 13 May 2025
claims.correction: Rule took legal effect 13 Jan 2025; key compliance requirements were set for 15 May 2025. BIS announced non-enforcement on 13 May 2025, before the main compliance date, NOT before legal effect.
claims.stale_check: GAO 12 May 2026 changes how this should be described.

claims.claim: BIS issued the AI Diffusion Rule on 13 Jan 2025.
claims.correction: GAO describes rule as issued 15 Jan 2025 and effective 13 Jan 2025. Use 'effective 13 Jan 2025' or 'published/issued 15 Jan 2025'.

claims.claim: BIS announced rescission on 13 May 2025 and GAO ruled in May 2026 that the rule remained in the CFR until rulemaking completes.
claims.source: BIS press release 13 May 2025; GAO B-337935
claims.correction: Accurate. GAO: press release subject to Congressional Review Act; rule legally in effect until Commerce completed rulemaking.

claims.claim: Johor approved 51 data-centre projects worth RM183 billion, 17 operational.
claims.source: The Star/Bloomberg 26 Nov 2025; NST via MWA 26 Nov 2025

claims.claim: November 2025 Johor stopped approving lower-tier data centres.
claims.correction: Specify 'Tier 1 and Tier 2 data centres' not 'lower-tier'.

claims.claim: Planned DCs could absorb over half Johor's treated water, 808 MLD vs 1,511 MLD.
claims.correction: Don't state as primary-verified SPAN figure. 808 MLD may refer to applications, not Johor-specific approved projects. Later: 384 MLD projected 2030, 2,001 MLD total consumption.

claims.claim: YTL, ByteDance and GDS have Johor data-centre footprints.
claims.source: YTL Data Centers MY locations; MIDA on BDC/ByteDance MY06 Johor 20 Oct 2022; MIDA on GDS Johor 29 Apr 2022
claims.correction: For ByteDance: link to BDC's MY06 Sedenak project rather than implying direct ownership.

claims.claim: Malaysia made US-origin AI chips subject to a Strategic Trade Permit on 14 July 2025 under STA 2010 Section 12 catch-all.
claims.source: MITI 14 Jul 2025; Strategic Trade Controller Directive No. 1/2025
claims.correction: Use 'high-performance' or 'advanced' AI chips of US origin, not all US-origin AI chips.

claims.claim: MITI said in March 2025 it had no findings of Nvidia chips reaching Malaysia.
claims.correction: Timestamp: 'as of 6 Mar 2025, Tengku Zafrul said there were no findings or reports indicating the alleged transfer had occurred.'

claims.claim: STA 2010 caps unlicensed-export fines at RM10M, or RM20M with intent, under Section 9.
claims.source: MITI Industry Guideline for Directive No. 1/2025
claims.correction: For July 2025 AI-chip directive: Section 12 unlisted items, not Section 9. MITI guideline: corporate penalties up to RM10M for unintentional/without-knowledge non-compliance and up to RM20M for intentional/with-knowledge.

claims.claim: Bridge Data Centres swapped a Chinese tenant in February 2026, replacing 68.4 MW with Zenlayer.
claims.source: The Edge MY/Bloomberg Apr 2026; DCD 9 Apr 2026
claims.correction: Attribute as 'Bloomberg reported, citing a February lender memo'. Underlying memo not verified.

claims.claim: Malaysia wrote its US-chip permit in July 2025, five months after the Singapore charges.
claims.correction: 27 Feb 2025 to 14 Jul 2025 is about 4.5 months. 'Roughly five months' acceptable; 'five months' precise should be corrected.

claims.claim: The permit exists, Bridge changed tenants, Johor tightened approvals, and the remaining test is whether anyone gets named.
claims.source: MITI 14 Jul 2025; NST/MWA 26 Nov 2025; Edge MY/Bloomberg Apr 2026
claims.correction: Permit and Johor tightening well supported. BDC swap not primary-verified. 'Naming' shouldn't imply only governance test.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: three on 27 Feb 2025 (a Singaporean, a permanent resident, a Chinese national)
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Permanent-resident detail not supported. Two Singaporeans and one Chinese national.
Rule legally effective from 13 Jan 2025. BIS announced non-enforcement before 15 May 2025 compliance date, not before legal effect.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Planned DCs could absorb over half Johor's treated water. SPAN proposed DC water demand of 808 MLD against Johor treated water consumption 1,511 MLD.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Primary SPAN dataset not found. Comparison via secondary reporting may mix applicant or broader demand with Johor-only treated-water consumption.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Planned DCs could absorb over half Johor's treated water.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Risks overstating Johor-specific burden without distinguishing proposed demand, approved projects, operating projects, allocated water, and projected 2030 demand.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Bridge Data Centres swapped a Chinese tenant in February 2026, replacing 68.4 MW with Zenlayer.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Relies on Bloomberg citing private lender memo. Memo not verified; no direct company confirmation.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: STA 2010 Section 9 — corporate fine up to RM10M for unlicensed export; up to RM20M with intent
cardinal_sin_flags.why: July 2025 AI-chip control framed by MITI as Section 12 catch-all/unlisted-item directive. Penalty statement should cite MITI guideline for Directive No. 1/2025.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: No Malaysian operator faces charges yet.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Absence of public charges cannot be proven by a single primary source. Should be date-stamped as a public-record search finding.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: No Malaysian enforcement release found as of 18 May 2026.

omitted_facts: The fourth Singapore charge on 2 Apr 2026 was against Lim Jenny, CFO of Aperia International, and concerned alleged conspiracy with Alan Wei and Aaron Woon to defraud Dell.
Singapore authorities did not confirm the servers contained export-controlled Nvidia chips; Shanmugam: 'may contain'.
GAO 12 May 2026 decision: BIS May 2025 press release did not itself complete rescission; AI Diffusion Rule remained legally in effect until rulemaking completed.
MITI July 2025 directive concerns advanced/high-performance AI chips of US origin, not every US-origin AI chip.
Water-demand claim needs cleaner denominator. Secondary reporting later cited Johor-specific figures that differ.
BDC tenant-swap claim should be Bloomberg-reported, not independently primary-verified. DCD: could not independently verify.
Transaction value in secondary coverage: ~US$390M or S$519M. Currency precision needed if added.
source_assessment: Strong source base for Singapore enforcement, US export-control status, and Malaysia's July 2025 AI-chip control: SPF, MHA, Federal Register, BIS, GAO, MITI, Parliament/Hansard, statutory materials. Weaker: Johor water demand, Johor project-count provenance, BDC tenant-swap evidence, absence of Malaysian charges. Source range institutionally broad but top-down; lacks water regulator raw data, Johor official documents, direct court charge sheets, affected operators' responses, opposition/NGO/community perspectives.
overall_assessment: Directionally reliable on core chronology: Singapore charged individuals, Singapore said servers may have contained Nvidia chips and went to Malaysia, Malaysia later imposed a Section 12 strategic-trade control on advanced US-origin AI chips, US AI Diffusion Rule's legal status became complicated after GAO May 2026 decision. However, contains publication-risk issues: one incorrect nationality/residency detail, incorrect/misleading 'paused before it took effect' statement, weakly sourced water-demand comparison, imprecise STA penalty citation, unverified BDC tenant-swap detail stated too firmly. Publishable only after tightening claims, adding date-stamped caveats, and downgrading non-primary evidence to attributed reporting.
